                Employment and Earnings is prepared by the
            Division of Industry Employment Statistics and the
            Division of Employment and Unemployment Anal-
            ysis in collaboration with the Division of Special
            Publications. The data are collected by the Bureau
            of the Census (Department of Commerce), State
            Employment Security Agencies, and State Depart-
            ments of Labor in cooperation with the Bureau of
            Labor Statistics. A brief description of the co-
            operative statistical programs of the BLS with
            these agencies is presented in the Explanatory
            Notes. The State agencies are listed on the inside
            back cover.                                                                        CALENDAR OF SPECIAL FEATURES                       {

                         Employment and Earnings
                         Vol. 26 No. 1 January 1979

                         Editors: Chester L. Green, Gloria P. Green, Marc Weinstein




                                    Editor's Note

                                        It is the annual practice of the Bureau of Labor Statistics to recalculate the seasonal adjustment
                                    factors for unemployment and other labor force series derived from the Current Population Survey
                                    (household survey) to incorporate the experience of the previous year. These revisions are usually
                                    published in conjunction with January data in the February issue of this publication. However, due to
                                    a speed-up in computer processing, it was possible to recalculate the seasonal adjustment in time
                                    for publication of revised data in this issue. Thus, Tables 1, 2,33-53, and 67 reflect revised seasonally
                                    adjusted data.
                                        Revised data for the entire 1974-78 revision period for a broad range of labor force series, a
                                    description of the current seasonal adjustment procedures, and seasonal adjustment factors to be
                                    used to calculate the overall unemployment rate during 1979 will appear in the February 1979 issue.

                                                                      Explanatory Notes

                                    These explanatory notes provide information on the concepts, methodology, and scope of Household Data
                                    (A tables), Establishment Data (B, C, and D tables), and State and Area Unemployment Data (E table) pub-
                                    lished in Employment and Earnings.



                                                                               Introduction

                   The statistics in this periodical are compiled from two major            Employment
                sources: (1) Household interviews, and (2) reports from employers.
                                                                                            Coverage. The household survey definition of employment com-
                    Data based on household interviews are obtained from a sample           prises wage and salary workers (including domestics and other
                survey of the population 16 years of age and over. The survey is            private household workers), self-employed persons, and unpaid
                conducted each month by the Bureau of the Census for the Bureau             workers who worked 15 hours or more during the survey week
                of Labor Statistics and provides comprehensive data on the labor            in family-operated enterprises. Employment in both agricultural
                force, the employed and the unemployed, including such charac-              and nonagricultural industries is included. The payroll survey
                teristics as age, sex, race, family relationship, marital status, occupa-   covers only wage and salary employees on the payrolls of non-
                tion, and industry attachment. The survey also provides data on the         agricultural establishments.
                characteristics and past work experience of those not in the labor
                force. The information is collected by trained interviewers from a          Multiple jobholding. The household approach provides information
                sample of about 56,000 households, representing 614 areas in                on the work status of the population without duplication, since
                1,113 counties and independent cities, with coverage in 50 States           each person is classified as employed, unemployed or not in the
                and the District of Columbia. The data collected are based on the           labor force. Employed persons holding more than one job are
                activity or status reported for the calendar week including the 12th        counted only once and are classified according to the job at which
                of the month.                                                               they worked the greatest number of hours during the survey week.

                signed to provide industry information on nonagricultural wage              Unpaid absences from jobs. The household survey includes among
                and salary employment, average weekly hours, average hourly                 the employed all persons who had jobs but were not at work during

                are based on payroll reports from a sample of establishments em-            tion, labor-management dispute, or because they were taking time off
                ploying over 30 million nonagricultural wage and salary workers.            for various other reasons, even if they were not paid by their em-

                actions occurring during the entire month.                                      For a comprehensive discussion of the differences between

                                                                                            and Payroll Surveys," Monthly Labor Review, December 1969. Re-

                    The household and establishment data supplement one another,            Labor Statistics.
                each providing significant types of information that the other can-
                not suitably supply. Population characteristics, for example, are
                readily obtained only from the household survey whereas detailed            Hours of work
                industrial classifications can be reliably derived only from establish-
                ment reports.                                                                  The household survey measures hours actually worked whereas
                     Data from these two sources differ from each other because of          the payroll survey measures hours paid for by employers. In the
                differences in definition and coverage, sources of information,             household survey data, all persons with a job but not at work are
                methods of collection, and estimating procedures. Sampling vari-            excluded from the hours distributions and the computations of

                        There are two types of errors possible in an estimate based on a       in the CPS.
                    sample survey—sampling and nonsampling. The standard errors                   Sampling error. Since the estimates are based on a sample, they
                    provided primarily indicate the magnitude of the sampling error.          may differ somewhat from the figures that would have been ob-
                    They also partially measure the effect of some nonsampling errors         tained if a complete census had been taken using the same schedules,
                    in response and enumeration but do not measure any systematic             instructions, and enumerators. The standard error is primarily a
                    biases in the data.                                                       measure of sampling variability, that is, of the variation that
                        Nonsampling errors. The full extent of nonsampling error is           occurs by chance because a sample rather than the entire popula-
                    unknown, but special studies have been conducted to qualify some          tion is surveyed. The chances are about 68 out of 100 that an
                    sources of nonsampling error in the CPS as discussed below. The           estimate from the survey differs from a complete census figure
                    effect of nonsampling error should be small on estimates of relative      by less than the standard error. The chances are about 95 out of
                    change, such as month-to-month change. Estimates of monthly               100 that the differences would be less than twice the standard
                    levels would be more severely affected by the nonsampling error.          error.
